text: Sodium-ion batteries have emerged as a frontrunner in electrochemical energy storage, yet achieving high energy density remains difficult due to the significant structural strain induced during (de)sodiation. To address this, we developed a P2-layered cathode, Na<sub>0.7</sub>Li<sub>0.03</sub>[Mg<sub>0.15</sub>Li<sub>0.07</sub>Mn<sub>0.75</sub>]O<sub>2</sub> (NMLMO), featuring a unique dual-site lithium substitution where Li ions occupy both transition-metal (TM) and alkali-metal (AM) sites. Integrating theoretical modeling with experimental insights, we demonstrate that TM-site lithium facilitates Na-O-Li electronic configurations that trigger robust oxygen anionic redox, significantly boosting capacity. Simultaneously, AM-site lithium acts as a structural "pillar" within the LiO<sub>6</sub> prisms, suppressing detrimental phase transitions and stabilizing the lattice. Consequently, the NMLMO cathode achieves a high specific capacity of 266 mAh g<sup>-1</sup> while maintaining a nearly zero-strain characteristic across a broad voltage window of 1.5–4.6 V. These results underscore dual-site substitution as a powerful strategy for overcoming the inherent trade-off between capacity and stability in next-generation battery materials.
